This is conservative by design, but it has a compounding effect when combined with a signal threshold of 0.050: the spread has to be both large in absolute terms and sustained over multiple days before any signal fires. In a low cross-sectional volatility environment like mid-to-late 2025, neither condition was frequently met, which is a key reason the strategy executed only one trade in 15 months. The fix is not simply to lower the threshold or raise the KF responsiveness in isolation; both parameters need to be co-optimized, because a more responsive KF (say dW/dV = 0.005 or 0.01) produces a noisier spread that requires a recalibrated threshold to avoid excessive false signals. This interaction between the filter and the threshold was not modeled in the optimization grid, which searched over thresholds with KF responsiveness fixed, and including KF_DW_SCALE as a third optimization dimension is the single highest-priority improvement for a future version of this strategy.

### 11.7 Within-Cluster Momentum Was Ignored, and It Cost Returns

The strategy treats all ETFs within a cluster as interchangeable: once the Cyclical cluster is selected, every ETF in that group receives an equal share of the cyclical allocation. This completely ignores the fact that individual sectors within a cluster can diverge dramatically from one another. During the test period, XLK was the clear standout within the Cyclical cluster, driven by the AI investment theme, while other cyclical sectors like XLE and XLB lagged significantly. A strategy that rotates into the Cyclical cluster without distinguishing between its members will always capture the average, never the best. An improvement would be to add a within-cluster momentum layer: rank ETFs inside each cluster by recent return momentum and tilt the allocation toward the top performers rather than splitting equally. This would have meaningfully increased participation in the technology rally without requiring any change to the cluster structure or signal logic, and it is a natural next iteration that preserves the core architecture while adding a second level of return-seeking behaviour.

### 11.8 The 80/20 Cluster Allocation Should Have Been Optimized, Not Fixed

The 80/20 split between the selected and non-selected cluster was chosen as a diversification rule: always keep some exposure to both sides to avoid concentration risk. In practice, this rule costs conviction. When the strategy signals Momentum Cyclical, it puts 80% in the Cyclical cluster and 20% in Defensive, meaning it is always partially hedged against its own call. A trading model that has generated a signal should act on that signal with appropriate sizing, not automatically diversify away 20% of it regardless of signal strength. The 80/20 was never optimized as a parameter, which means it was an assumption masquerading as a design choice. Including the cluster allocation split in the optimization grid, allowing it to range from 60/40 to 100/0 depending on what the training data supports, would have produced a more honest and potentially more profitable position sizing rule. In markets where conviction is rewarded, playing it safe by construction is just a slower way to underperform.

### 11.9 Every Failure Is a Step Forward

A year ago, building a quantitative trading strategy from scratch, with clustering, Kalman filtering, rolling signals, parallel optimization, and an execution loop with dividends and transaction costs, would have been an entirely foreign task. The FIN 440 model failed on fixed parameters, outlier sensitivity, and no significance testing. This model addressed all three of those failures by design and introduced new failures of its own: infrequent trading, a threshold calibrated to the wrong regime, and a complexity premium that was not earned in the test period. That progression, from not knowing what the mistakes are, to making intentional design choices that solve the last round of mistakes, to identifying a new set of more sophisticated problems, is what learning actually looks like. The model did not succeed in beating passive benchmarks, but the understanding of why it failed is worth more than a model that worked by accident.

### 11.10 Conclusion

I have improved drastically over my FIN 440 model, but there is still a ways to go. This is a testament that in order to succeed in anything, you need to try, try, and try again. Building a model is like a free lottery ticket, except the odds of striking gold increase every time as I continue to improve on past mistakes.
